coredata.dc:title: Efficient room temperature afterglow in nitrogen doped carbon dots triggered by steric hindrance effect
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jing
Wang, Yafang
Lu, Xiaona
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention in the construction of metal free afterglow materials. Most of the precursor materials used for the preparation of CDs are hazardous chemicals and the afterglow decay time is very short. The current improvements were made by modifying the reaction time and embedding dopants. Two series of boronic acid-based carbon dots (CDs/BA) and N-doped boronic acid-based carbon dots (NCDs/BA) have been successfully prepared. The luminescence results indicate that the boronic acid caused boron carbon (B-C) bond can reduce the energy gap (Δ EST) between the lowest singlet state (S1) and triplet state (T1), which leads to the promotion of intersystem crossing (ISC) between S1 and T1 and the increase of the number of triplet excitons. Additionally, morphological characterization as well as the luminescence performance of NCDs/BA indicate that the steric hindrance effect has another effect on the activating of room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) of boronic acid-based CDs, which could increase the skeleton stiffness of CDs and effectively protect the excited triplet state of CDs from non-radiative deactivation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Afterglow
Steric hindrance effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and optical properties of CDs/BA
Morphology and optical properties of NCDs/BA

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the production of CDs/BA (a) and NCDs/BA(b).
TEM plots of 0.5-CDs/BA (a), 1-CDs/BA (b), 3-CDs/BA (c) with the inset figures of particle size distribution.
Photographs of X-CDs/BA with 365 nm UV lamp on and off.
FT-IR spectra of X-CDs/BA and BA.
Emission spectra with solution concentration (a) photos of sample before and after turning off the UV lamp (b) and FT-IR spectra (c) of CDs/BA aqueous solution.
Excitation spectra and emission spectra of BA.

Excitation spectra, emission spectra and afterglow emission spectra of 0.5-CDs/BA (a), 1-CDs/BA (b), 3-CDs/BA (c). Energy plot of PL for 0.5-CDs/BA (d), 1-CDs/BA (e), 3-CDs/BA (f). The energy level structure diagram of 0.5-CDs/BA (g), 1-CDs/BA (h), 3-CDs/BA (i).
Photographs of X-NCDs/BA with 365 nm UV lamp on and off.
Emission spectra (a) and afterglow emission spectra (b) of X-NCDs/BA when the excitation light source is switch off.
The energy level structure diagram of 0.5-NCDs/BA (a), 1-NCDs/BA (b), 3-NCDs/BA (c), 4-NCDs/BA (d), 5-NCDs/BA (e).

head.ce:title: Efficient room temperature afterglow in nitrogen doped carbon dots triggered by steric hindrance effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China University of Geosciences, Beijing, 100083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, China University of Geosciences, Beijing, 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention in the construction of metal free afterglow materials. Most of the precursor materials used for the preparation of CDs are hazardous chemicals and the afterglow decay time is very short. The current improvements were made by modifying the reaction time and embedding dopants. Two series of boronic acid-based carbon dots (CDs/BA) and N-doped boronic acid-based carbon dots (NCDs/BA) have been successfully prepared. The luminescence results indicate that the boronic acid caused boron carbon (B-C) bond can reduce the energy gap (Δ EST) between the lowest singlet state (S1) and triplet state (T1), which leads to the promotion of intersystem crossing (ISC) between S1 and T1 and the increase of the number of triplet excitons. Additionally, morphological characterization as well as the luminescence performance of NCDs/BA indicate that the steric hindrance effect has another effect on the activating of room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) of boronic acid-based CDs, which could increase the skeleton stiffness of CDs and effectively protect the excited triplet state of CDs from non-radiative deactivation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials not only emit fluorescence when absorbing energy, but also emit with longer wavelength phosphorescence after removing the excitation light source. There are two types of traditional RTP. One is metal complexes containing heavy atoms such as Ir and Pt, and the other is pure organic compounds containing atoms such as C and H. Metal complexes are limited in their applications in many fields due to their poor processing performance, high cost, and strong metal toxicity [1]. The spin orbit coupling of pure organic compounds is weak, and triplet excitons are prone to non-radiative deactivation through molecular vibration, rotation, and other pathways which leads to the short afterglow decay time [2]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop new non-metallic RTP materials with low toxicity, easy preparation, and long afterglow decay time.

Fluorescent carbon was firstly reported by the research group of Xu in 2004 [3], which opened the door to the world of carbon dots (CDs). Since then, graphene quantum dots (GQD) [4], carbon quantum dots (CQD) [5], carbon nanodots (CND) [6], and carbon polymer dots (CPD) [7] have been successively produced. These quasi-spherical shapes with the size less than 10 nm are referred as CDs [8]. Due to the excellent optical performance, biocompatibility, photostability, low toxicity, hydrophilicity, and environmental friendliness [9–12], CDs have also shown potential application in fields such as biomedical [13], sensing [14], anti-counterfeiting [15], and information encryption [16]. And the preparation method of CDs is simple, low-cost, and easy to functionalization [17], so the use of CDs to construct RTP materials has attracted numerous interests.

In general, CDs powder cannot exhibit good afterglow performance because it is easily deactivated by O2 molecules in the air [18–21]. There are two ways to obtain efficient RTP CDs. One is to enhance the intersystem crossing (ISC) to populate effective triplet excitons. The other is to stabilize the triplet excitons by strengthening the rigidity of CDs to reduce non-radiative transitions and external quenching [2]. Thus, two strategies for producing RTP CDs have been carried out. One is embedding CDs into various matrices [22–29], and the other is doping CDs with heteroatoms [27]. The first strategy can limit the vibration and rotation of the emission center of CDs by constructing a hydrogen bonding network and strengthening CDs rigidity, thereby suppressing non-radiative transitions and improving its afterglow performance [28,29]. The second strategy can reduce the energy gap (Δ EST) between the lowest singlet states (S1) and triplet states (T1) of CDs, which leads to the promoting ISC of excitons [30].

However, the afterglow decay time of RTP CDs is still short and afterglow images with a smart phone is only 8 s after the UV lamp was switched off [31]. This greatly limits the application of RTP CDs in many aspects. In addition, hazardous chemicals such as m-phenylenediamine [32] and triphenylamine [19] are mostly chosen when selecting dopants, which makes the synthesis process of RTP CDs complex and increases experimental costs [33]. However, there are few literatures on the synthesis of RTP CDs using urea as dopants. Therefore, developing low-cost, environmentally friendly and ultralong room temperature afterglow is still a challenge for synthesizing RTP CDs.

Here, using BA as the matrix, the negative electronegativity of boron atoms is utilized to promote the formation of p-π conjugated systems and reduce the ΔEST between the single and triplet states [34]. At the same time, citric acid was chosen as the precursor material to synthesize boronic acid-based carbon dots (CDs/BA) composite materials with afterglow properties. Based on previous research [35], the FT-IR spectra and PL spectra were combined for the first to analyze the influence of chemical bonds on the afterglow characteristics of samples. Then, low-cost and harmless urea was chosen as the dopants for CDs to synthesize nitrogen doped boronic acid-based carbon dots (NCD/BA) composites. In order to analyze the effect of N atoms on the afterglow characteristics of the samples the microscopic morphology and optical properties of the samples were characterized.

A series of X-CD/BA (X = 0.5, 1, 3 h) composites and X-NCD/BA (X = 0.5, 1, 3, 4, 5 h) composites have been synthesized by using a hydrothermal method. As shown in Fig. 1 , citric acid and urea were used as precursors with variations of the reaction time. Then, the boric acid was completely dissolved into CDs or NCDs after ultrasonic treatment, and the final sample was obtained by being heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Finally, an agate mortar was used to grind the sample into fine powder for later use.

The microscopic morphology and particle size of samples were observed by H-8100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Scientific). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and afterglow decay curves were recorded by FluoroMax-4 (Horiba) with a 365 nm UV lamp as the excitation light. The iPhone 14 was used to capture the afterglow images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of CDs/BA
ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic morphology structure of X-CDs/BA composites are shown in Fig. 2 . It is observed that CDs are dispersed in BA matrix with quasi-spherical shapes which confirms the successful preparation of the composites. In addition, with the increase of precursor reaction time, the particle size of X-CDs/BA does not show any significant changes. The average size of the 0.5-CDs/BA is 2.75 nm, the average size of the 1-CDs/BA is 2.65 nm and the average size of the 3-CDs/BA is 2.5 nm.

The afterglow images obtained by the smart phone varies with the precursor reaction times of the samples as shown in Fig. 3 . Sample 1-CDs/BA presents the longest persistent luminescence time of 10 s, while the other two samples only have 8 s. To elucidate the cause of the variations of afterglow, optical performance tests were conducted on the samples.

The materials used for synthesizing CDs/BA are citric acid and BA, therefore, the composite materials contain four elements: C, H, O, and B, which can be demonstrated by the FT-IR spectra of the samples, as shown in Fig. 4 . It can be observed that there are several identical characteristic absorption peaks which are at 3211 cm−1, 1415 cm−1, 1193 cm−1 and 884 cm−1 respectively. The first two absorption peaks are attributed to -OH and B-O stretching vibrations [36]. The peak at 1193 cm−1 is originated from an asymmetrically stretched oxygen atoms connecting the trigonal boron atoms [37]. The peak at 884 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of B-OH [33]. However, it is worth noting that there is a new characteristic absorption peak at 948 cm−1 compared with BA sample, which should be originated from B-C [38]. This indicates that B-C can attach CDs to BA, and enhance the rigidity structure of CDs. From Fig. 4, it can be seen that the B-C bond content of 1-CDs/BA is higher than the other two samples, indicating that the structure of 1-CDs/BA is more stable and has better afterglow characteristics [35,39].

PL emission spectra and FT-IR spectra of the sample aqueous solution were observed, as shown in Fig. 5 . The sample with aqueous solution does not exhibit afterglow characteristics (Fig. 5b). There is a blue fluorescence with the emission center at 442 nm when the aqueous solution of sample is irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp. The fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution is dependent with the concentration. Higher concentration leads to the higher the fluorescence intensity of the aqueous solution. Correspondingly, as the concentration of the solution increases, the absorption peaks of the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 5c) at 1415 cm−1 and 1193 cm−1 also increase. Therefore, the stretching vibrations of B-O and the asymmetric stretching oxygen atoms connecting the triangular boron atoms may affect the emission peak of the sample at 442 nm.

However, compared with the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 4a) of powder samples of CDs/BA, the aqueous solution of the sample does not show absorption peaks at 948 cm−1 and 884 cm−1. This indicates oxygen molecules in water disrupt the hydrogen bonding network between CDs and BA, which leads to the breakdown of the BA matrix used for embedding CDs and therefore there is no RTP characteristics of CDs/BA.
In order to analysis the other functional groups of the CDs/BA, the PL of BA and CDs/BA were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 6 , under 245 nm UV lamp excitation, BA exhibits an emission peak at 395 nm.

The PL and afterglow emission spectra of CDs/BA are shown in Fig. 7 a–c. Under 365 nm UV lamp excitation, CDs/BA have a wide emission band with two emission centers in the blue light range, which may be caused by the different functional groups of the sample. The emission spectra and afterglow emission peaks of samples are different, which demonstrates the processes of afterglow are different with photoluminescence.

After converting the emission spectra into the energy plot, the spectral curves can be deconvoluted into two peaks by the Gaussian method [40,41], as shown in Fig. 7d–f. The bending vibration of B-OH may affect the emission peak at 395 nm. The stretching vibrations of B-O and the asymmetric stretching oxygen atoms connecting the triangular boron atoms may affect the emission peak of the sample at 442 nm. However, due to the increase of B-C bonds in 1-CDs/BA, the energy levels of the sample undergo a sudden change [35], which leads to the shift of emission wavelength from 442 nm to 464 nm. Similarly, converting the peak positions of the afterglow emission spectra of X-CDs/BA into energy plot give us the hint of the energy gap. The energy level structure diagrams of the samples have been drawn, as shown in Fig. 7g–i. The Δ EST of 1-CDs/BA is the smallest among the samples, which is 0.39 eV, indicating that increase of boron-carbon bond can effectively reduce the Δ EST and consequently facilitating the ISC between S1 and T1 [42].

Based on the above discussion, it can be inferred that attaching CDs to the BA matrix can effectively protect triplet excitons from quenching. Moreover, the different reaction times of precursors result in varying amounts of boron carbon bonds in the sample. The increase of boron carbon bonds in 1-CDs/BA can reduce the Δ EST of 1-CDs, providing more triplet excitons for 1-CDs, resulting in longer afterglow time of up to 10 s for 1-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of NCDs/BA
ce:section.ce:para: The strategy for constructing RTP CDs is not only to embed CDs into the matrix to promote the rate of ISC and improve the rigidity of CDs, but also to construct the hydrogen bonding network of CDs through doping with heteroatoms. Because N and C have similar atomic radii, urea was chosen as a dopant to provide N source for CDs. However, according to previous research, the reaction time of the precursor has an impact on the afterglow performance of the sample. Therefore, increasing the reaction time of the precursor again to explore the effect of N doping on RTP CDs.

As the reaction time of the precursor increases, the afterglow time of X-NCDs/BA significantly increases, as shown in Fig. 8 . When the reaction time of the precursor has exceeded 3 h, the afterglow time of the sample has slightly decreased. Among these samples, 3-NCDs/BA exhibit a green afterglow with a duration of up to 12 s. This is the sample with the longest afterglow persistent luminescence time of RTP CDs constructed using BA as the matrix to date [35,37,38,43]. This result indicates that the reaction time of the precursor affects the afterglow characteristics of X-NCDs/BA.

Similarly, the materials used for synthesizing NCDs/BA are citric acid, urea, and BA. Therefore, the composite material contains five elements: C, H, O, N, and B, which can also be demonstrated by the FT-IR spectra of the samples, as shown in Fig. 11 . Five samples exhibit characteristic absorption peaks at 2880-2987 cm−1, which are attributed to the bending vibration of C-H [44]. A broad characteristic absorption peak appears at 1393-1521 cm−1, which can be attributed to the C=N bonds [21]. The absorption peak of the sample at 1193 cm−1 gradually shifts to 1247 cm−1 with the increase of precursor reaction time. The peak at 1247 cm−1 is attributed to C-OH. As the reaction time of the precursor increases, the absorption peak of C-OH gradually decreases. The peak at 1064 cm−1 is originated from C-O/C-N [30,45]. This result indicates that N atoms were successfully incorporated into CDs. The reaction time of the precursor affects the degree of completion of NCD in the synthesis process. Generally speaking, one assumes that the longer the reaction time, the more complete of the reaction between substances [46]. Therefore, the number of functional groups in the samples varies on the reaction time of the precursor. As the reaction time of the precursor increases, the functional groups increase, including C-H, C-OH, C-O, and C-N, which form a tighter hydrogen bonding network and improves the rigidity of CDs. However, if the reaction time of the precursor is too long, excessive reaction will occur, which will affect the number of surface functional group on the surface of NCDs.

The reaction time of the precursor can affect the number of functional groups in the samples, and may also promote the crosslinking polymerization/carbonization of carbon core, determining the core size of final CDs [47]. To prove it, the TEM of the samples were tested, as shown in Fig. 12 . X-NCDs/BA are uniformly dispersed in BA matrix with quasi-spherical shapes. The particle sizes of the samples were 1.43 nm, 1.61 nm, 2.75 nm, 1.91 nm, and 1.77 nm, respectively. As the precursor reaction time increased, the particle size of NCDs first increased and then decreased.

From the above results, it can be inferred that with the increase of reaction time, the number of functional groups on the surface of the sample and the particle size of the sample show a phenomenon of increasing and then decreasing. The spatial hindrance effect of the sample also changes accordingly [48]. When the particle size of the sample is large, the steric hindrance effect also increases. The more functional groups on the surface of the sample, the more obvious the steric hindrance effect becomes. This leads to a tighter hydrogen bonding network in the sample and improves rigid structure of the sample, which can effectively protect the excited triplet state of carbon dots from non-radiative deactivation.

In summary, 1-CDs/BA and 3-NCDs/BA with long room temperature afterglow properties were synthesized, and their afterglow time by the smart phone reach 10 s and 12 s, respectively. It is because that embedding CDs into the BA matrix, can enhance the rigidity of CDs and effectively protect triplet excitons from quenching. An increase in the number of the B-C bonds can reduces the Δ EST of CDs/BA, which promotes the ISC between S1 and T1 and increases the number of effective triplet excitons in CDs/BA, thereby improving its afterglow properties. The introduction of N atoms did not affect the energy gap of X-NCDs/BAs, but as the reaction precursor time increases, the spatial hindrance effect of the sample first increases and then decreases. Therefore, steric hindrance effect is an important factor in activating the RTP of NCDs/BA, which affects the hydrogen bonding network and rigidity of NCDs/BA, thereby affecting their afterglow performance.
Jing Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yafang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xiaona Lu: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
